summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the unveiling of Office 12's ribbon interface at PDC 2005, arguing that it represents the death of the traditional main menu that has been a cornerstone of graphical user interfaces since the Apple Lisa. He explains that the ribbon consolidates all commands into a single, context-sensitive tabbed palette, eliminating the need to hunt through menus and toolbars. Atwood predicts this paradigm shift will spread across Microsoft's product line, third-party applications, and other operating systems, just as the toolbar did after Word introduced it in the early 90s. He also fact-checks Microsoft's press release, noting that toolbars appeared much earlier than 1997.
key_points: The Office 12 ribbon interface eliminates the traditional drop-down main menu entirely, replacing it with context-sensitive tabbed palettes
UI innovations in Office historically spread across the entire software industry, as the toolbar did after its introduction in Word
The main menu has been fundamental to the WIMP (Windows, Icons, Menus, Pointer) interface paradigm since the Apple Lisa era
Microsoft's own press materials incorrectly dated the toolbar's introduction to 1997, when it actually appeared in Word 1.1a and Excel 4.0 in the early 90s
money_quotes: We had just witnessed the death of the main menu.
That's huge. The main menu has been a cornerstone of the WIMP interface since way back in the days of the Apple Lisa.
There's no longer a stack of task panes and menus and toolbars to look through. There's just one place to look for commands.
The 'ribbon' is a similar paradigm shift. Eventually we'll all be using these tabbed palettes with nary a drop-down menu in sight.
I expect the traditional WIMP main menu to go the way of the dodo soon after the release of Vista and Office 12 in 2006.
I don't know what kind of crack those MS presspass guys are smoking...
key_insight: Major UI innovations from Microsoft Office, like the toolbar before it and the ribbon now, tend to reshape the entire software industry's interface conventions.
